docs.title: Introduction
AI and Windows Granite Workshop
docs.text:
Welcome to our workshop! In this workshop we'll be using the open-sourced IBM Granite AI foundation models for a number of use cases that demonstrates the value of generative AI.

The goals of this workshop are:

  • Installing Open Source AI tools on your own Windows 11 laptop (Mac instructions to follow soon) to run AI locally.
  • Running LLM models via command line
  • Running LLM models with a UI interface
  • Try to improve results with Retrieval-Augmented Generation
  • Running with a multimodal LLM

docs.title: About this workshop
docs.text:
The introductory page of the workshop is broken down into the following sections:

  • Agenda
  • Compatibility
  • Technology Used
  • Credits

docs.title: Agenda
docs.text: Pre-Work Prerequisites for the workshop Lab 1: Command-Line Ollama Lab 1 Lab 2: Using Open-WebUI Interface Lab 2 Lab 3: Retrieval-Augmented Generation Lab 3 Lab 4: Graphics and MultiModal LLM Lab 4

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
This workshop has been tested on the following platforms:

  • Windows 11 (Ideally with at least 16GB of memory, but 8GB memory can work with smaller models.
  • (MacOS instructions to follow soon)

docs.title: Technology Used
docs.text:
• IBM Granite Models
  • Open Source Ollama
  • Open source Open-WebUI

docs.title: Credits
docs.text:
• James Busche
  • Yi-Hong Wang

docs.title: Command-Line Ollama
1.1 List the available models downloaded
docs.text: ollama list

docs.title: 1.2 Run one of the models, for example:
docs.text: ollama run granite3.3:2b

docs.title: 1.3 Issue it some questions, try it out.  For example:
docs.text:
list the first 10 presidents of the united states

create an easy to make chocolate chip cookie recipe

give me some places to visit while I'm in Silicon Valley

docs.title: 1.4 Clear the context
docs.text: /clear

docs.title: 1.5 Give it clear instructions to change it's behavior.  For example:
docs.text:
Answer questions from now on in Spanish

Answer questions like a pirate

docs.title: 1.6 Exit out of the current model
docs.text: /bye

docs.title: 1.7 List and then start another model:
docs.text:
ollama list
ollama run granite3.2-vision:latest

docs.title: 1.8 Issue it some questions, try it out.  For example:
docs.text:
create an easy to make chocolate chip cookie recipe

list the ingredients as well as 5 optional additions

docs.title: 1.9  Exit out of the current model:
docs.text: /bye

docs.title: Using Open-WebUI Interface
2.1 Get into the python environment if you're not already there:
2.2 Start the Open WebUI
docs.text: open-webui serve

docs.title: 2.3 Hit the localhost with the browser:
docs.text: http://localhost:8080

docs.title: 2.4 Pick a model to use, for example:
docs.text: granite3.3:8b

docs.title: 2.5 Issue it some questions, try it out.  For example:
docs.text:
list the first 10 presidents of the united states

create an easy to make chocolate chip cookie recipe

give me some places to visit while I'm in Silicon Valley

docs.title: 2.6 Clear the context by clicking on "New Chat"
2.7 Give it clear instructions to change the behavior.  For example:
docs.text:
Only answer questions in rhyme
What was domesticated first, a dog or a cat?

docs.title: 2.8 Clear the context by clicking on "New Chat"
Retrieval-Augmented Generation
3.1 In the Open-WebUI, switch to the smallest granite model:  granite3.3:2b
3.2 Click on "New Chat" so the context is clear
3.3 Ask it for:
docs.text:
List the past and current CEOs of IBM corporation from oldest to latest
The answer isn't correct.

docs.title: 3.4 Click on "New Chat" to clear the context
3.5 Click on the "+" and then Upload files
docs.text: Upload the IBM.txt from: IBM.txt: resources/IBM.txt

docs.title: 3.6 Now ask it our question about the CEOs of IBM:
docs.text: List all the past and current CEOs of IBM corporation from oldest to latest

docs.title: 3.7 We can also find and download to pdf from Wikipedia:
docs.text: History of IBM - On the right, click on "Tools" and Click on "Download as PDF"

docs.title: 3.8 Then use this History_of_IBM.pdf as a RAG by clicking on the + and adding "History_of_IBM.pdf" as a file.
3.9 Then use the Open-WebUI to ask more questions about IBM.
docs.text:
Who is the first female CEO of IBM?
The answer should be Ginni Rometty

docs.title: Graphics and MultiModal LLM
4.1 Get into the python environment if you're not already there:
4.2 Start the Open WebUI
docs.text: open-webui serve

docs.title: 4.3 Hit the localhost with the browser:
docs.text: http://localhost:8080

docs.title: 4.4 Pick a model to use, for example:
docs.text: granite3.2-vision:2b

docs.title: 4.5 Issue it some questions, try it out.  For example:
docs.text:
list the first 10 presidents of the united states
create an easy to make chocolate chip cookie recipe

docs.title: 4.6 Clear the context by clicking on "New Chat"
4.7 Using another tab on the browser, search for a image.  For example:
docs.text: Kangaroo

docs.title: 4.8 In the Google search, click on the "Images" tab and right-click on one of the photos you like and select "Copy Image"
4.9 Using the Open-WebUI browser, do a CTRL-V to paste the copied image to the Open-WebUI and press enter. The Granite vision model will summarize what it sees from the image.
4.10 Go ahead and ask the AI more questions about the animal in the image.  For example:
docs.text: What is the lifespan for the animal in the photo?

docs.title: Pre-work
docs.text:
Assumptions:

These labs assume you have a Microsoft Windows 11 machine.

(For MacOS instructions, click Here: MacOS)

docs.title: 1.1 Download and Install Ollama for Windows:
docs.text:
Ollama for Windows

Install Ollama by double-clicking on the downloaded OllamaSetup.exe

And then clicking on "Install"

docs.title: 1.2 Open up a command prompt (Search for "CMD" and click on "Command Prompt")
1.3. Download at least 3 of the Granite models
docs.text:
ollama pull granite3.3:2b
ollama pull granite3.2-vision:2b
ollama pull granite3.3:8b
Note: We won't use them for this lab, but if you wanted to try some other LLM models, you can start with: 
ollama pull deepseek-r1:1.5b
ollama pull llama3.2:3b
Check out the entire Hugging face library of models at: https://ollama.com/library

docs.title: 1.4 Install Python 3.11.9 for Windows
docs.text:
python-3.11.9-amd64.exe

Double-click to install it

Note: While installing - make sure you check the box to "add the python.exe to path"

docs.title: 1.5 Close and re-open your command prompt and then check to make sure python is findable on the path by typing:
docs.text:
python --version
It should return: "Python 3.11.9"

If it doesn't find the python command, likely you forgot to check the box to put python in the path. No worry, just: 

Double-click on the downloaded python-3.1.9-amd64.exe file again and this time chose "Modify".  
Click "next" 
Put a check next to the box: "Add Python to the environnment variables" 
Click "Install"

docs.title: 1.6 Create a python virtual environment in your command prompt:
docs.text: python -m venv --upgrade-deps --clear venv

docs.title: 1.7 Activate your python environment by typing:
docs.text: "venv/Scripts/activate.bat"

docs.title: 1.8 Install the Open-WebUI
docs.text: pip install open-webui

docs.title: 1.9 Start up open-webui:
docs.text: open-webui serve

docs.title: 1.10 Once the open-webui starts, use a browser to login to open-webui:
docs.text:
http://localhost:8080

Enter a name, email address and password - these are stored locally.

docs.title: Cleaning up after the lab is complete:
docs.text:
Remove all the models you don't want: 

ollama list
ollama rm granite3.3:2b granite3.3:8b granite3.2-vision:2b

docs.title: Uninstall ollama:
docs.text: In the search bar, search for Settings → Apps → Installed Apps and search for Ollama and click on Uninstall

docs.title: Stop and remove open-webUI:
docs.text: In the command line where open-webui is running, do a ctrl-C to stop the "open-webui serve"

docs.title: Then, deactivate your python environment session by typing:
docs.text: "venv/Scripts/deactivate.bat" to exit the python virtual environment

docs.title: If you want to remove the virutal environment completely, you could issue a:
docs.text: rmdir /s venv

docs.title: Mac Instructions to install Ollama and Open-WebUI
docs.text:
Assumptions:

These instructions assume you have a MacOS machine.

docs.title: 1.1 Download and Install Ollama for MacOS:
docs.text:
On macOS, you can use Homebrew to install with:

brew install ollama

docs.title: 1.2 Start the Ollama server. You will leave this running during the workshop.
docs.text: ollama serve

docs.title: 1.3 In another terminal window, pull down the Granite models you will want to use in the workshop. Larger models take more memory to run but can give better results.
docs.text:
ollama pull granite3.3:2b
ollama pull granite3.2-vision:2b
ollama pull granite3.3:8b
Note: We won't use them for this lab, but if you wanted to try some other LLM models, you can start with: 
ollama pull deepseek-r1:1.5b
ollama pull llama3.2:3b
Check out the entire Hugging face library of models at: https://ollama.com/library

docs.title: 1.4 Install Python 3.11.9 for MacOS
docs.text:
pyenv install 3.11

pyenv global 3.11.9


pyenv -V
It should return now 3.11.9

docs.title: 1.5 Create a Python virtual environment:
docs.text: python3 -m venv --upgrade-deps venv

docs.title: 1.6 Activate the virtual environment:
docs.text: source venv/bin/activate

docs.title: 1.7 Install Open-WebUI
docs.text: pip install open-webui

docs.title: 1.8 Start up Open-WebUI in a terminal window. You will leave this running during the workshop.
docs.text: open-webui serve

docs.title: Prerequisite
docs.text:
This workshop uses ......

Please follow the installation links below to set up your working environment:

  • prerequisite 1
  • prerequisite 2

docs.title: Clone the workshop repository
docs.text:
Various parts of this workshop will require the configuration files from the workshop repository. Use the following command to download the whole repository 

git clone https://github.com/IBM/intro-ai-llm-workshop.git
cd intro-ai-llm-workshop

Or download the repository as a zip file, unzip it and get into the intro-ai-llm-workshop-main directory.
